easy.solution:
My Blog Post

By John Doe | March 15, 2025

Welcome to my blog! Today I want to share some thoughts about writing clean, semantic HTML. Understanding block-level elements is the foundation of good web development.

Block elements like headings, paragraphs, and divisions form the structural backbone of every web page. They stack vertically, creating a clear visual hierarchy that makes content easy to read and navigate.

"Good design is as little design as possible. Less, but better — because it concentrates on the essential aspects." — Dieter Rams

When you use block elements correctly, your HTML becomes more meaningful, accessible, and maintainable. Semantic elements like <header>, <main>, and <footer> add further clarity.

Thanks for reading! Check back next week for more tips on web development best practices.

My Website

Home About

Article Title

Article content goes here.

Footer content
medium.solution:
Web Dev Insights

Exploring Modern Web Development

HomeArticlesTutorialsAboutContact

Understanding Semantic HTML

By Sarah Developer | March 20, 2025

What Are Semantic Elements?

Semantic HTML elements clearly describe their meaning to both the browser and the developer. Unlike generic <div> tags, semantic elements like <article> and <section> convey structural meaning.

Why Use Them?

Semantic elements improve accessibility by helping screen readers navigate content. They also boost SEO by providing search engines with better content understanding.

Best Practices

Use <header> for introductory content, <nav> for navigation, <main> for primary content, <article> for self-contained pieces, <section> for grouping, <aside> for related content, and <footer> for closing information.

Understanding the CSS Box Model

By Alex Mentor | March 2025

The Four Layers

Every block element in CSS consists of content, padding, border, and margin. Understanding how these layers interact is crucial for layout design.

Box-Sizing Property

The box-sizing property controls how width and height are calculated:

/* Default */
* { box-sizing: border-box; }
"The box model is the foundation of CSS layout. Master it, and you master the web."

Stay tuned for more advanced layout techniques in our next lesson.

Semantic HTML5 Elements

Why Semantics Matter

Using semantic elements improves accessibility, SEO, and code maintainability. Search engines and screen readers rely on semantic structure to understand content.
